text: it myself.” A string of caws from wheeling crows seemed to scold me for the sentiment. That they’d become nocturnal was strange as the vanished moon. “We’ve come far to get our payday, so it’s good that it has our coin.” “It is no longer a city. The currency there you perhaps will not recognize.” “Another thing we reckon is you’re the one who carried our friends off. Man like you seems wily enough. Strong enough too.” “No. You see the changed stars. And those among them see you.” With that the blood man rose. He turned and that sightless black cave regarded me deeper than his good eye ever could. His face was carved with lines and valleys and puckered scars. A short covering of dark hair, like a monk from the far south, and no beard or even the shadow of one. He passed into the dark like oilsmoke. His stone remained by the fire. The others looked to me but I shook my head. Quiet drew down. The horses calmed and the crows sought, we hoped, bleaker scenes. After some talk we elected to forgo lashing ourselves together. The blood man had our trust, thin as it was. # My worn bladder had grown small the last of those sixty-eight years and carried blood in it. On those nights when we joined our wrists, the others made me relieve myself before we all lay down in a heap. I’d chew wiltgrass to ease my discomfort. But that night I was unbound. The pain built to a gnaw and I awoke staring up at that infinite dome and its dwindling points of light. Like the earth waited to be swallowed into a great mouth of black. The Dippers had slunk across the sky and hung in the middle of it like watchful eyes beneath an insect’s antennae. It turned bitter cold. The shivers wouldn’t quit and I walked off a proper ways past the firelight. Unbuckling myself took longer than it had any business to. I braced a hand against the bark of a stunted tree and pissed as my bones shook in that dark with the crackles of the snoring men and the dying fire behind and nothing ahead. But those sounds fell away and the night folded around me. “Guenard,” something whispered, and the lilt it rode was that of my wife who had borne me four strong daughters. My Isa who lay with the eating sickness a hundred miles west in another world altogether. The stream of urine shut off with a fierce burning pinch and I gasped. Her voice drifted out at the edge of my ears, saying I had enough gold for her doctors and lacked only the comfort of her bosom and her fingers trailing through my hair, which she would soon wash in the warm brook coursing deeper in the wood. She waited to lull me with the song of the lady Adelín who cleansed the world with the tears of her lovers, to which we’d swayed at our wedding. Obscured in the fog of my brain I knew it was the blood man, for he had spoken my true name just hours before. But my wife knew that song well, and what passed for my soul was already singing it with her. Breath plumed from my mouth. I buttoned my trousers and stepped forward, the rest of my urine running hot down my leg. Leaves rustled above though the wind had long ceased. “I’m in the trees,” Isa whispered, and a hand fell on my shoulder. Its fingers were long enough to clamp over my breast and the heart that lay knocking beneath it. “Easy here, swordsman,” the blood man said into my ear. He pulled me back and only my helpless moan continued into the dark. I turned and could only stare at my hand, which had lifted to strike him. “Get your tricks off me, wizard.” I waited for my heart to unclench. A knot of thunder gathered in it. The blood man wore a faint nimbus of light. He removed his hand and laid it upon my chest. The pain loosened itself at once. “Now you know what befell your companions and why I cannot sleep.” The skin around his socket flexed and the good eye had regained its milky yolk color. “What speaks as my wife?” He watched me from the unknowable, godless face. In his other hand he clutched the black stone. “Those who have long peered through keyholes. Eyes that have studied the mites crawling along the skin of the world and the motes shifting in its sun.” “How is the city changed?” I asked him. The questions at my tongue were many and I seized at the one that sat in the distance upon our road. Talk of stars was beyond my reach. “I told you it is no city. It now has a key fitted into it.” “We aim to keep on.” I could speak for the six of us. With Mantalbo gone the governor of El Porvenir might think to break our contract, though we’d sewn up our end with the three dead men whose heads we carted. But there was bison to sell at the least. My Isa was sick, and we had children with children, a nest of mouths to feed. “Build up the fire, Guenard. Keep out of the night.” I did as the blood man told and he sat and watched us in our uneasy rest. # We came down out of the mountains and rode into the desert, six wayward pilgrims in that vast waste and the blood man riding behind. He clung bareback to Julo’s spotted horse as the sun clawed up from the earth’s rim to blacken us upon the cracked pan. El Porvenir awaited our return to Mexico, wavering in the distant haze. We hoped to reach it before midday. Our bellies were full and sloshing with water, the horses renewed and content
